title: Antivirus & Protection
subtitle: Your Digital Immune System — How to Stay Safe Online!
overview: Knowing that malware exists is only half the battle — the other half is knowing how to stop it. In this session, students explore the full toolkit of digital protection: how antivirus software works, what firewalls do, why software updates matter, how strong passwords and 2FA defend accounts, and how safe browsing habits form the most powerful line of defence. Students leave with practical, actionable skills to protect themselves, their families, and their devices.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — antivirus UI screenshots, firewall diagram, password strength examples, 2FA demonstration.

gallery.caption: Safe browsing checklist — habits that block most online threats before they start

keyTopics: Antivirus Software — Detects, quarantines, and removes malware using signature databases and behavioural analysis
Signature Detection — Comparing file code against a database of known malware patterns; fast but cannot catch brand-new (zero-day) threats
Heuristic Analysis — Looking for suspicious behaviour patterns rather than known signatures; can detect new threats not yet in the database
Real-Time Protection — Antivirus actively monitors file activity, downloads, and processes as they happen rather than only during scheduled scans
Quarantine — Isolating a suspected malicious file in a restricted area so it cannot run or spread while awaiting review or deletion
Firewall — A security system (hardware or software) that monitors and filters network traffic based on rules, blocking unauthorised access
Software Updates & Patches — Updates close known security vulnerabilities that attackers exploit; unpatched software is an open door
Strong Password — Long (12+ characters), random mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols; unique for every account
Password Manager — Software that generates and securely stores unique strong passwords for every account (e.g. Bitwarden, 1Password)
Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) — A second verification step beyond the password: SMS code, authenticator app, or biometric
HTTPS — HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure; encrypted connection between browser and website (look for the padlock icon)
Phishing Awareness — Recognising fake emails, websites, and messages designed to steal credentials; the human layer of defence

activity.title: Personal Cyber Security Audit + Protection Setup
parts: Computer or tablet with internet access
Personal Cyber Security Audit Worksheet (printed or digital)
Access to a free password strength checker (e.g. passwordmonster.com)
steps: Part 1 — Defence Layers Map: Students draw a castle diagram with 6 protective walls — labelling each layer: Antivirus, Firewall, Software Updates, Strong Passwords, 2FA, and Safe Browsing. Discuss what each layer protects against

Part 2 — Antivirus Exploration: Open Windows Defender (or installed antivirus) on the classroom computer. Identify: real-time protection status, last scan date, virus definition update date. Discuss: what would happen if definitions were 6 months old?

Part 3 — Password Strength Test: Students test 5 sample passwords ('password123', 'P@ssw0rd', 'correct-horse-battery-staple', 'a8#Kx2!mQr', 'ilovecricket2024') on passwordmonster.com. Rank them by strength and discuss why length beats complexity alone
Part 4 — Pwned Check: Students type their school email (or a sample email) into haveibeenpwned.com to check if it appears in known data breaches. Discuss what to do if a breach is found

Part 5 — 2FA Setup Demo: Teacher demonstrates enabling 2FA on a Google account using Google Authenticator. Students identify which of their own accounts offer 2FA and list the top 3 they would enable first

Part 6 — Safe Browsing Quiz: Show students 6 website URLs and email snippets. They must identify which ones are safe and which are phishing attempts — checking for HTTPS, correct domain spelling, urgent language, and suspicious sender addresses

Part 7 — Personal Security Action Plan: Each student writes a 5-point personal cybersecurity improvement plan — specific actions they will take this week (e.g. 'Enable 2FA on Gmail', 'Change my Instagram password to 16+ characters', 'Update my phone OS')

Challenge: Students research and present on one real-world data breach (e.g. Adobe 2013, Yahoo 2016, LinkedIn 2021) — how many accounts affected, what data was stolen, and what users should have done differently

quiz.q: What is the primary purpose of Antivirus software?
options: To speed up your computer by removing old files
To detect, quarantine, and remove malware from a device
To block all incoming internet traffic
To back up your files automatically
quiz.explanation: Antivirus software is your digital immune system! It scans files, monitors processes, and checks downloads for known and suspicious malware. When a threat is found, it quarantines or deletes it before damage is done.

quiz.q: What is Signature Detection in antivirus software?
options: The antivirus asking you to digitally sign detected threats
Comparing files against a database of known malware code patterns — if a match is found, the file is flagged as malicious
Monitoring program behaviour for suspicious activity
Verifying the digital signature on legitimate software downloads
quiz.explanation: Signature detection is like a fingerprint database! Every known malware has a unique code pattern (signature). The antivirus scans files and compares them to millions of known signatures. Instant match = instant quarantine. This is why updating definitions daily matters — new malware = new signatures needed.

quiz.q: What is Heuristic Analysis, and why is it important?
options: A method of detecting malware by scanning only compressed files
Analysing code or behaviour for suspicious patterns to detect NEW threats that don't yet have a signature in the database
A technique for recovering quarantined files
Scanning only files downloaded from the internet
quiz.explanation: Heuristic analysis fights unknown threats! It looks for suspicious patterns — like code that tries to hide itself, replicate, or access sensitive system areas — even if the malware is brand new and has no signature yet. Essential for catching zero-day threats.

quiz.q: What is Real-Time Protection in an antivirus program?
options: A scan that runs exactly at midnight every day
Continuous monitoring of file activity, downloads, and processes as they happen — blocking threats instantly rather than waiting for a scheduled scan
Protection that only activates when you are browsing the internet
A live connection to the antivirus company's server
quiz.explanation: Real-time protection is always on! Unlike scheduled scans that check files periodically, real-time protection watches every file that is opened, downloaded, or executed. If a threat is detected the moment a malicious file tries to run — it's blocked instantly.

quiz.q: What does it mean when antivirus software 'Quarantines' a file?
options: It permanently deletes the file from the hard drive
It isolates the suspected file in a restricted area where it cannot run or spread, until the user decides to delete or restore it
It sends the file to the antivirus company for analysis
It encrypts the file to prevent it from spreading
quiz.explanation: Quarantine is a safe holding area! The file is moved somewhere it cannot execute or infect other files. This is safer than immediate deletion — sometimes legitimate files get flagged as false positives, and quarantine lets you restore them if needed.

options: It scans files for viruses and removes them
It monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules, blocking unauthorised access
It backs up files to prevent data loss
It encrypts all files stored on the hard drive
quiz.explanation: A firewall is a network gatekeeper! Think of it as a security guard at a building entrance — it checks every packet of data trying to enter or leave, allows legitimate traffic, and blocks suspicious or unauthorised connections. Works alongside antivirus — different threats, different tools.

quiz.q: What is the difference between a Software Firewall and a Hardware Firewall?
options: Software firewalls are free; hardware firewalls always cost money
A software firewall runs as a program on a single device; a hardware firewall is a physical device protecting an entire network
Hardware firewalls block viruses; software firewalls block hackers
There is no difference — they do exactly the same job
quiz.explanation: Both protect, but at different levels! Software firewall (like Windows Firewall) protects the individual device it runs on. Hardware firewall (like a router with built-in firewall) sits between the internet and ALL devices on the network, protecting everything at once — used in offices and schools.

quiz.q: Why are Software Updates and Security Patches critically important for protection?
options: Updates add new features like emojis and themes
Updates patch known security vulnerabilities in software — unpatched systems are actively targeted by malware and hackers
Updates are only important for gaming performance
Updates train the antivirus database with new signatures
quiz.explanation: Unpatched = open door! WannaCry ransomware exploited a Windows vulnerability that had already been patched — but millions hadn't updated. Attackers scan for unpatched systems. Updates close the holes before attackers can use them. Enable automatic updates wherever possible!

options: Password123
p@55w0rd
correct-horse-battery-staple-river
Qw3rty!
quiz.explanation: Length beats complexity! 'correct-horse-battery-staple-river' has 35 characters — even using only lowercase letters and hyphens, it would take centuries to brute-force. Short passwords with symbols like 'Qw3rty!' are cracked in seconds. Long passphrases are both strong AND memorable!

quiz.q: What is a Password Manager and why should everyone use one?
options: A notebook where you write down all your passwords
Software that generates and securely stores unique strong passwords for every account, so you only need to remember one master password
A person who manages passwords for a company
A browser feature that saves passwords in plain text
quiz.explanation: Password managers solve the human problem! Most people reuse weak passwords because strong unique ones are hard to remember. A password manager generates random 20-character passwords for every site and stores them encrypted. You only need to remember one strong master password.

options: Using two different passwords for the same account
A second verification step beyond the password — such as a code sent to your phone or a biometric scan — that stops attackers even if they have your password
Logging in from two different devices simultaneously
A method of encrypting data twice for extra security
quiz.explanation: 2FA = two locks on the door! Even if an attacker steals your password, they still can't get in without the second factor (your phone, fingerprint, or authenticator app). Enabling 2FA on email and bank accounts is one of the single most effective security steps you can take.

quiz.q: What does the padlock icon and HTTPS in a browser address bar tell you?
options: The website is owned by a trusted government organisation
The connection between your browser and the website is encrypted — data sent and received cannot be read by third parties on the same network
The website has passed an antivirus scan
The website is safe to download files from
quiz.explanation: HTTPS = encrypted connection! It means your data is scrambled in transit — someone intercepting your WiFi traffic cannot read your passwords or personal information. Always look for HTTPS before entering any sensitive data. HTTP without the S = no encryption = visible to attackers on the same network.

quiz.q: What is a Zero-Day vulnerability, and why can't antivirus always protect against it?
options: A vulnerability that has existed for zero days — meaning it is newly patched
A security flaw that is unknown to the software developer and has no patch yet — attackers exploit it before defenders even know it exists
A virus that activates at midnight (zero hour)
A type of firewall rule with zero exceptions
quiz.explanation: Zero-day = unknown to defenders! The name means the developer has had 'zero days' to fix it. Signature-based antivirus cannot detect zero-day malware because there's no signature yet. Heuristic analysis and behaviour monitoring are the best defences against zero-day attacks.

quiz.q: A student receives an email claiming their bank account has been locked and they must click a link immediately to verify their details. What should they do?
options: Click the link immediately — it sounds urgent and important
Reply to the email asking for more information
Recognise it as a likely phishing attack — do NOT click the link. Go directly to the bank's official website by typing the URL manually, or call the bank
Forward the email to all their friends as a warning
quiz.explanation: Classic phishing! Urgency, fear, and a link are the three red flags. Real banks NEVER ask for credentials via email links. Always go to the official website by typing the URL yourself. Clicking the link could take you to a fake login page that steals your credentials, or install malware.

quiz.q: What is the MOST effective combination of protections for a personal computer?
options: Antivirus only — it catches everything
Firewall only — it blocks all threats at the network level
Defence in depth: antivirus + firewall + automatic updates + strong unique passwords + 2FA + safe browsing habits — multiple layers working together
Turning off WiFi when not in use — no internet means no threats
quiz.explanation: No single tool is perfect! Defence in depth means using multiple overlapping layers — antivirus catches malware, firewall blocks network attacks, updates close vulnerabilities, strong passwords protect accounts, 2FA stops stolen password use, and safe browsing avoids threats altogether. Together they cover each other's blind spots.
